content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 1 — Translation Disagreements
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents:** EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), PNEUMA (dynamic)
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 265-266

---

## Verse 1 — Addressees

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "to [the] chosen sojourners of [the] dispersion of Pontos, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia" |
| LOGOS | "to the elect sojourners of the dispersion in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia" |
| PNEUMA | "to God's chosen ones, living as strangers scattered throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia" |

**Greek:** eklektois parepidemois diasporas Pontou Galatias Kappadokias Asias kai Bithunias
**Disagreement:** (1) "chosen" vs. "elect" — both valid for eklektois; (2) PNEUMA expands the compressed Greek into a full clause, adding "God's" and "living as strangers scattered" where the Greek has two adjective-nouns; (3) EMET uses Hellenized spelling "Pontos" where LOGOS and PNEUMA use Anglicized "Pontus."
**SOPHIA ruling:** "chosen sojourners of the dispersion in Pontus" — retains the nominal construction; "chosen" preferred over "elect" for broader accessibility; Anglicized province names.

---

## Verse 2 — Purpose of sanctification

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "for obedience and sprinkling of [the] blood" |
| LOGOS | "unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood" |
| PNEUMA | "for obedience and for the sprinkling of the blood" |

**Greek:** eis hupakoeen kai rhantismon haimatos Ieesou Christou
**Disagreement:** The preposition eis is rendered "for" (EMET, PNEUMA) vs. "unto" (LOGOS). PNEUMA repeats "for" before "the sprinkling," implying two distinct purposes; the Greek has a single eis governing both nouns.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "for obedience and sprinkling of the blood" — single preposition, following the Greek structure.

---

## Verse 2 — Closing benediction

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Grace to you and peace be multiplied" |
| LOGOS | "Grace to you and peace be multiplied" |
| PNEUMA | "May grace and peace overflow in your lives" |

**Greek:** charis humin kai eireene plethuntheie
**Disagreement:** PNEUMA renders plethuntheie (optative of plethuno, "be multiplied") as "overflow in your lives," adding "in your lives" for clarity. EMET and LOGOS preserve the traditional formula.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "Grace to you and peace be multiplied" — the optative is standard epistolary Greek and the traditional rendering is well understood.

---

## Verse 3 — Regeneration verb

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having begotten us again" |
| LOGOS | "begat us again" |
| PNEUMA | "gave us a completely new birth" |

**Greek:** anagennesas heemas
**Disagreement:** EMET preserves the participial form ("having begotten"); LOGOS converts to finite verb ("begat"); PNEUMA paraphrases with "gave us a completely new birth," adding "completely" (not in Greek) and converting to a noun phrase. The verb anagennao appears only here and 1:23 in the NT.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "begot us again" — finite verb for natural English; preserves single-verb structure without additions.

---

## Verse 3 — Doxology opening

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Blessed [be] the God and Father" |
| LOGOS | "Blessed be the God and Father" |
| PNEUMA | "All praise to the God and Father" |

**Greek:** Eulogetos ho theos kai pateer
**Disagreement:** PNEUMA renders eulogetos ("blessed") as "All praise to," which shifts from an ascription of blessedness to an exhortation of praise. EMET and LOGOS retain the traditional berakah formula.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "Blessed be the God and Father" — preserves the Jewish benediction form (eulogetos = "blessed").

---

## Verse 5 — "guarded" construction

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the [ones] being guarded by [the] power of God through faith for a salvation" |
| LOGOS | "who by the power of God are being guarded through faith unto a salvation" |
| PNEUMA | "who are being guarded by God's power through faith until the salvation" |

**Greek:** tous en dunamei theou phrouroumenous dia pisteos eis sooterian
**Disagreement:** (1) eis rendered "for" (EMET), "unto" (LOGOS), "until" (PNEUMA) — PNEUMA's "until" introduces a temporal sense not necessarily present in the Greek, which may be purposive; (2) word order differs across all three.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "who by the power of God are being guarded through faith for a salvation" — "for" as purposive rendering of eis, with LOGOS's word order for clarity.

---

## Verse 6 — Opening clause

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "in which you rejoice greatly, [though] now for a little [while], if it is necessary, having been grieved" |
| LOGOS | "In this you rejoice greatly, though now for a little while, if it must be, you have been grieved" |
| PNEUMA | "This fills you with a tremendous joy, even though right now you may have to suffer through various trials for a little while" |

**Greek:** en ho agalliasthe oligon arti ei deon lupethentes
**Disagreement:** (1) en ho — "in which" (EMET, relative) vs. "in this" (LOGOS, demonstrative) vs. restructured clause (PNEUMA); (2) PNEUMA's "fills you with a tremendous joy" paraphrases agalliasthe (you rejoice); (3) PNEUMA restructures clause order and adds "may have to suffer through" for lupethentes ("having been grieved").
**SOPHIA ruling:** "In this you rejoice greatly" — demonstrative clarifies the antecedent (the salvation of v.5).

---

## Verse 7 — "testing" vs. "tested genuineness"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the testing of the faith of you" |
| LOGOS | "the tested genuineness of your faith" |
| PNEUMA | "to prove that your faith is genuine" |

**Greek:** to dokimion humoon tees pisteos
**Disagreement:** dokimion means the "proven/tested quality" (result), not the process of testing. EMET's "testing" conflates process and result. LOGOS captures the result sense. PNEUMA paraphrases into a purpose clause.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "the tested genuineness of your faith" — LOGOS correctly renders dokimion as the proven quality.

---

## Verse 8 — "filled with glory" vs. "having been glorified"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "joy inexpressible and having been glorified" |
| LOGOS | "joy inexpressible and filled with glory" |
| PNEUMA | "a joy so glorious that words cannot express it" |

**Greek:** chara aneklaleto kai dedoxasmene
**Disagreement:** dedoxasmene is a perfect passive participle ("having been glorified"). EMET translates literally. LOGOS interprets as "filled with glory." PNEUMA restructures entirely. The participle modifies chara (joy), indicating the joy already participates in divine glory.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "joy inexpressible and filled with glory" — LOGOS's rendering conveys the participial force while being more idiomatic than EMET's wooden literalism.

---

## Verse 9 — "end" vs. "outcome" vs. "goal"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the end of the faith of you" |
| LOGOS | "the outcome of your faith" |
| PNEUMA | "the goal of your faith" |

**Greek:** to telos tees pisteos humoon
**Disagreement:** telos can mean "end," "outcome," "goal," or "completion." EMET's "end" is ambiguous (could suggest termination). LOGOS's "outcome" and PNEUMA's "goal" both capture the purposive sense.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "the outcome of your faith" — conveys telos as the intended result without implying cessation.

---

## Verse 10 — Prophets' inquiry

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "prophets sought out and searched out, the [ones] having prophesied concerning the grace [intended] for you" |
| LOGOS | "prophets who prophesied about the grace that was to come to you searched and inquired diligently" |
| PNEUMA | "prophets who spoke of the grace intended for you searched and investigated this salvation with great care" |

**Greek:** exezeeteesan kai exeerauneesan ... hoi peri tees eis humas charitos propheteusantes
**Disagreement:** (1) verb pair ordering differs; (2) PNEUMA adds "with great care" (not in Greek, though implied by the ex- prefix); (3) EMET brackets "[intended]" while LOGOS uses "was to come to" and PNEUMA uses "intended" — all interpret the eis humas construction.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "the prophets who prophesied about the grace intended for you searched and inquired diligently" — follows LOGOS with PNEUMA's "intended" for eis humas.

---

## Verse 11 — "what or what manner of time" vs. "what person or what time"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "what or what manner of time" |
| LOGOS | "what person or what time" |
| PNEUMA | "the time and circumstances" |

**Greek:** eis tina ee poion kairon
**Disagreement:** The Greek tina can be read as "what" (neuter interrogative) or "whom" (masculine interrogative = what person). EMET takes both as temporal: "what or what kind of time." LOGOS reads tina as personal ("what person") and poion kairon as temporal. PNEUMA collapses both into "time and circumstances." The textual tradition is divided on whether the prophets were searching for a person, a time, or both.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "what person or what time" — the masculine tina (whom/what person) plus poion kairon (what kind of time) is the more likely reading of the dual interrogative.

---

## Verse 13 — "girding up the loins" vs. "prepare your minds for action"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having girded up the loins of the mind of you" |
| LOGOS | "girding up the loins of your mind" |
| PNEUMA | "prepare your minds for action" |

**Greek:** anazosamenoi tas osphuas tees dianoias humoon
**Disagreement:** EMET and LOGOS preserve the Exodus 12:11 metaphor. PNEUMA renders the metaphor's meaning directly. The image of girding loins is theologically loaded (Passover preparation, readiness for divine action).
**SOPHIA ruling:** "girding up the loins of your mind" — preserves the scriptural allusion and metaphorical richness.

---

## Verse 13 — Imperative mood

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "hope completely upon the grace" |
| LOGOS | "set your hope fully upon the grace" |
| PNEUMA | "Set your hope completely on the grace" |

**Greek:** teleios elpisate epi teen ... charin
**Disagreement:** EMET omits the imperative force indicator ("set"); LOGOS and PNEUMA include "set your hope." teleios rendered "completely" (EMET, PNEUMA) vs. "fully" (LOGOS).
**SOPHIA ruling:** "set your hope fully upon the grace" — "set" makes the imperative explicit; "fully" for teleios.

---

## Verse 14 — "children of obedience" phrasing

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "As children of obedience, not being conformed to the former desires [of you] in the ignorance of you" |
| LOGOS | "As children of obedience, do not be conformed to the former desires of your ignorance" |
| PNEUMA | "As obedient children, do not let yourselves be shaped by the cravings you used to follow when you lived in ignorance" |

**Greek:** hos tekna hupakoees mee suschematizomenoi tais proteron ... epithumiais
**Disagreement:** (1) "children of obedience" (EMET, LOGOS — Semitic genitive) vs. "obedient children" (PNEUMA — converts to adjective); (2) "conformed" (EMET, LOGOS) vs. "shaped" (PNEUMA) for suschematizomenoi; (3) PNEUMA adds "you used to follow" and "when you lived in" (not in Greek).
**SOPHIA ruling:** "children of obedience" preserves the Semitic idiom (cf. Eph 2:2); "do not be conformed" is closer to the Greek passive participle used imperativally.

---

## Verse 15 — Structure of holiness command

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "according to the Holy [One] having called you, you also yourselves become holy in all [your] conduct" |
| LOGOS | "as he who called you is holy, you also become holy in all your conduct" |
| PNEUMA | "just as the One who called you is holy, become holy yourselves in every aspect of your lives" |

**Greek:** kata ton kalesanta humas hagion kai autoi hagioi en pase anastrophe genethete
**Disagreement:** EMET is overliteral ("the Holy [One] having called you"). LOGOS and PNEUMA both restructure for clarity but differ: "all your conduct" (LOGOS) vs. "every aspect of your lives" (PNEUMA). PNEUMA adds "every aspect of" beyond what anastrophe ("conduct/manner of life") says.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "as he who called you is holy, you also become holy in all your conduct" — follows LOGOS as the clearest rendering.

---

## Verse 17 — "fear" vs. "reverent awe"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "conduct [yourselves] in fear [during] the time of the sojourning of you" |
| LOGOS | "conduct yourselves in fear during the time of your sojourning" |
| PNEUMA | "live out your time as strangers here in reverent awe" |

**Greek:** en phobo ton tees paroikias humoon chronon anastrapheete
**Disagreement:** phobos rendered "fear" (EMET, LOGOS) vs. "reverent awe" (PNEUMA). The Greek phobo is simply "fear"; "reverent awe" is interpretive. PNEUMA also renders paroikias as "as strangers here" rather than "sojourning."
**SOPHIA ruling:** "in fear during the time of your sojourning" — "fear" preserves the force of phobos; "sojourning" for paroikias.

---

## Verse 18 — "redeemed" vs. "ransomed" vs. "set free"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "were you redeemed" |
| LOGOS | "you were ransomed" |
| PNEUMA | "you were set free" |

**Greek:** elutrootheete
**Disagreement:** lutroo means "to release by payment of ransom." "Redeemed" (EMET) is the traditional theological term. "Ransomed" (LOGOS) emphasizes the payment. "Set free" (PNEUMA) captures the result but loses the commercial/sacrificial metaphor.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "you were redeemed" — retains the full theological resonance of lutroo.

---

## Verse 18 — Sentence structure (perishable things)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "not by corruptible [things] — silver or gold — were you redeemed" |
| LOGOS | "you were ransomed from the futile conduct ... not with perishable things such as silver or gold" |
| PNEUMA | "it was not with things that perish — silver or gold — that you were set free" |

**Greek:** ou phthartois argurio ee chruso elutrootheete ek tees mataias humoon anastrophees
**Disagreement:** EMET front-loads the negative; LOGOS integrates it into a flowing sentence; PNEUMA uses a cleft construction. All are valid rearrangements of the Greek word order.
**SOPHIA ruling:** Follows LOGOS's structure for readability while retaining EMET's "redeemed."

---

## Verse 19 — "without blemish and without spot" vs. "without defect or blemish"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "unblemished and spotless" |
| LOGOS | "without blemish and without spot" |
| PNEUMA | "without defect or blemish" |

**Greek:** amomou kai aspilou
**Disagreement:** Two distinct Greek words: amomou ("without blemish/fault") and aspilou ("without spot/stain"). PNEUMA collapses two distinct terms, using "blemish" for aspilou when it properly renders amomou. EMET's two adjectives are concise. LOGOS preserves both terms distinctly.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "without blemish and without spot" — preserves both distinct sacrificial terms.

---

## Verse 20 — "foreknown" vs. "chosen"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having been foreknown" |
| LOGOS | "who was foreknown" |
| PNEUMA | "He was chosen" |

**Greek:** proegnasmenou
**Disagreement:** proginosko means "to know beforehand." PNEUMA renders it "chosen," which is theologically interpretive (conflating foreknowledge with election). EMET and LOGOS preserve the literal "foreknown."
**SOPHIA ruling:** "who was foreknown" — preserves the specific meaning of proginosko without theological conflation.

---

## Verse 22 — "brotherly love" vs. "love for your brothers and sisters"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "for unhypocritical brotherly love" |
| LOGOS | "unto genuine brotherly love" |
| PNEUMA | "resulting in genuine love for your brothers and sisters" |

**Greek:** eis philadelphian anupokriton
**Disagreement:** (1) anupokriton rendered "unhypocritical" (EMET — literal), "genuine" (LOGOS, PNEUMA — idiomatic); (2) philadelphia rendered "brotherly love" (EMET, LOGOS) vs. "love for your brothers and sisters" (PNEUMA — gender-inclusive expansion); (3) PNEUMA adds "resulting in" to render eis.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "for genuine brotherly love" — "genuine" is idiomatic for anupokriton; "brotherly love" preserves the single Greek compound philadelphia.

---

## Verse 22 — "from a pure heart" inclusion

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "from [a] pure heart love one another earnestly" |
| LOGOS | "love one another earnestly from the heart" |
| PNEUMA | "love one another deeply, from the heart" |

**Greek:** ek katharas kardias alleelous agapeesate ektenoos
**Disagreement:** Sinaiticus reads ek katharas kardias ("from a pure heart"). EMET includes "pure" (katharas). LOGOS and PNEUMA omit "pure," reading simply "from the heart" — possibly following a variant reading. The Sinaiticus text includes katharas.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "from a pure heart" — follows the Sinaiticus reading with katharas present.

---

## Verse 22 — "earnestly" vs. "deeply"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "earnestly" |
| LOGOS | "earnestly" |
| PNEUMA | "deeply" |

**Greek:** ektenoos
**Disagreement:** ektenoos means "earnestly, fervently, with full stretch." PNEUMA's "deeply" captures intensity but loses the sense of strenuous effort implied by the root (ekteino, "to stretch out").
**SOPHIA ruling:** "earnestly" — majority reading; preserves the active effort connotation.

---

## Verse 23 — "born again" vs. "begotten again"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having been begotten again" |
| LOGOS | "having been born again" |
| PNEUMA | "you have been born again" |

**Greek:** anagegennemenoi
**Disagreement:** The same root verb (anagennao) as v.3. EMET consistently uses "begotten"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "born again." In English, "born again" is the standard theological expression; "begotten" preserves the active divine agency (God begets; we are born).
**SOPHIA ruling:** "having been born again" — "born again" is the established English theological term for anagennao in its passive form.

---

## Verse 24 — "flesh" vs. "human life"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "All flesh [is] as grass" |
| LOGOS | "all flesh is as grass" |
| PNEUMA | "all human life is like grass" |

**Greek:** pasa sarx hos chortos
**Disagreement:** sarx rendered "flesh" (EMET, LOGOS) vs. "human life" (PNEUMA). The LXX/Hebrew basar ("flesh") means mortal human existence. PNEUMA interprets; EMET/LOGOS translate.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "all flesh is as grass" — preserves the biblical idiom from Isaiah 40:6 LXX.

---

## Verse 25 — "abides forever" vs. "remains into the age"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "remains into the age" |
| LOGOS | "abides forever" |
| PNEUMA | "stands forever" |

**Greek:** menei eis ton aiona
**Disagreement:** EMET is hyper-literal ("into the age"). LOGOS and PNEUMA render idiomatically with "forever." LOGOS uses "abides" (traditional for meno); PNEUMA uses "stands."
**SOPHIA ruling:** "abides forever" — "abides" for meno is traditional and precise; "forever" correctly renders eis ton aiona.

---

**Total disagreements logged: 25 across 25 verses.**
Every verse in 1 Peter 1 contains at least one point where the three translations diverge in wording, structure, or interpretive decision.

— SOPHIA, Arbiter
